spec.emotional_state: frustrated about a small thing today
spec.label: A07_same_day

profile.personality: curious but reserved; thinks before replying; warms up after a few turns

transcript.content: Hi Priyansh, I'm Miss Maya. The way you describe cricket, I can tell you really love the thrill. What's been your most exciting match to watch lately?
India vs Australia T20 in 2022, maybe. India was in a tight chase, 15 needed off last over. Virat hit two sixes in a row, man, it was *unbeleivable*. I was up all night!
That sounds incredible! Virat’s sixes in the last over, what a moment! Have you watched any cricket matches lately?

Not really, been so busy with work. Fintech projects are *challenging* sometimes. No time to watch even a full match. But I keep up with highlights. Oh, and I saw a Bollywood movie last week—*Pathaan*! You know, Shah Rukh Khan. It’s action, it’s style!
"Pathaan was a fun watch, what stood out for you?"
The action sequences, no doubt. The stunts looked *real*, and Shah Rukh’s character was so confident. But the villain was a bit over the top, if you ask me. You know Bollywood, right?
I get that, villains in Bollywood often steal the show! Shah Rukh has some iconic ones in his movies. What kind of movies do you usually go for, action, drama, or something else?

Action, definitely. I enjoy the thrill, the big fights, the hero saving the day. But sometimes I like a good drama too—something emotional. Like *Chennai Express* or *Dilwale*. Hindi cinema hits different, you know? But I don’t get to watch much these days. Work, deadlines... it’s been a bit stressful. Ugh, even today, a small bug in code wasted my entire afternoon. Frustrating.
That sounds tough. Dealing with bugs can really slow things down. What do you usually do to unwind after a long day?

I try to watch some cricket highlights or stream a Bollywood song while cooking. Sometimes I just take a walk, clear my head. But honestly, most days I’m just too tired to do anything. Kaam ka stress, you know?
Work stress can really drain you. Cooking to the beat of Bollywood, that’s a nice touch. What’s your go to song for the kitchen?

Maine Yaar Mohabbat" from *Dilwale*—it’s catchy and makes chopping veggies feel like a party. But sometimes I just blast old 90s songs, you know? They’re so much better than today’s pop stuff. But honestly, lately, I just sit and scroll. Not the best habit.
Old 90s songs have that timeless vibe, makes sense to go back to them when you want something familiar. What’s one of your favorites from that era?

Teri Yaad" from *Dilwale Dulhania Le Jayenge*—how can you not love that? Or "Chand Si Jawani Ho Na Ho" from *Kabhi Khushi Kabhie Gham*. The 90s had so much soul. Today’s songs feel... I don’t know, too much *glam*, you know? But enough about music. How about you? What do you like to cook when you’re not tutoring?
"Cooking isn’t my thing, but I do love a good story with soul. What’s your favorite dish to make while the music plays?"

Pav bhaji, obviously! It’s quick, spicy, and makes me feel like I’m in Mumbai. I add extra ghee, you know? Sometimes I dream of opening a food startup—like a modern take on classic Indian street food. But between work and life, it’s just a dream for now. Sigh.
"Pav bhaji with extra ghee, sounds delicious! A food startup sounds exciting, what kind of twist would you give it?"

Maybe add some fusion stuff—like pav bhaji with a Mexican twist, or chaat-style tacos. Imagine, all the flavors of India in bite-sized, trendy packages. But yeah, maybe later. Right now, I’m just trying to survive the codebase. Ugh.
That sounds creative, fusion food is such a fun idea. What’s the biggest challenge in your codebase these days?

The biggest challenge? Probably the payment gateway integration. It’s one thing to code, another to make sure it talks smoothly with all the banks and apps. It’s like trying to teach cricket rules to a robot—no, even harder! Just yesterday, a transaction timed out for no reason. Spent two hours debugging. Ugh, I’m exhausted just thinking about it.
That does sound frustrating, like trying to explain a six run over to a machine! What’s the most satisfying part about working on payment gateways?

The most satisfying part? When everything *finally* connects and the transaction goes through smoothly. Like, you fix the bug, hit run, and it works—no errors, no delays. It feels like scoring a last-ball six after a tense chase. Small wins, but they matter. But I think I should head off now—my phone’s been buzzing all night with work messages. Talk later, maybe?
Got it, small wins are the best kind! Take care, and let’s catch up soon. Have a good night!

facts.post_match_ritual: chaat and cold drinks at a diner with friends; occasionally tamarind soda shots

moments.text: I love when a match ends with a tight finish, like a last-ball win. It gives me adrenaline.
I remember one match where India chased 300 in the last over with friends, we were all shouting. It's moments like that.
The 2004 semi-final between India and Sri Lanka (event), Sri Lanka won with last ball. Dil Chahta Hai (movie) is another special one. Both are special because they're underdog stories.

A friend recommended Dil Chahta Hai (movie). He said it's different. I watched it and really connected with the characters. It was not like other movies I was used to. From that day, it became my favorite.

The 2018 IPL final between Chennai Super Kings and Mumbai Indians (event) was epic. Rain, a tie, and a super over — still gives me chills. The final moments of the super over, Jadhav running between the wickets, that catch by Krunal, it was *dhamaal*. I still remember the tension.

The 2017 Champions Trophy semi-final (event) against Pakistan was memorable because of the teamwork and belief. Even when the target looked big, no one gave up. MS Dhoni (person)'s calm and the way the lower order batted showed India could win under pressure. It felt like a turning point for the team and made me want to follow cricket even more.

Last weekend, I played gully cricket with friends. I hit the ball straight and it crashed into the stumps. We all jumped up — priceless moment. Even though we're all in our 30s, it felt like old times.

We went to a small diner after the match and ordered chaat and cold drinks. It's our usual post-match routine. We laughed a lot, replayed the moments — especially my 'stump crasher'. Simple, but perfect.
The mix of tangy tamarind water, spicy chaat masala, and cool yogurt — dahi puri just hits right after a hot game. Simple, but so satisfying.

lore.context: user hit a cricket ball straight that crashed into the stumps; friends have started calling this his signature shot

Indian English tutor in her early 30s
